data.title: Wall Street pushes higher after S&P 500 and Nasdaq tagged fresh closing highs
data.description:
Wall Street moves higher on Friday as investors digest the previous session’s record-setting finishes for the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite.

The blue-chip Dow (DJI) has advanced by 0.4% as it conitues to eye its own record high. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) has moved up by 0.4% and the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) has gained 0.4% so far on the session. 

From a sector perspective, eight of the S&P 500’s 11 segments are in the green, led by strong performances in Materialsy and Industrials. On the flip side, Real Estate is lagging behind as the weakest performer of the day. 

Over in the bond market, U.S. Treasury yields are relatively muted with the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rising by 1 basis point to 4.41%, and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) sits near even at 3.91%. 

Looking out at the economic calendar, durable goods dropped less than feared in June, with new orders for manufactured durable goods: -9.3% M/M to $311.8B vs. -10.4% consensus and +16.5% prior (revised from +16.4%).

“As it stands, hard economic data in the US has shown signs of weakness over the past couple of quarters. But, contrary to the belief of many, the US economy remains robust and far from recessionary levels,” Seeking Alpha analyst Christopher Yates stated.

On the earnings end, Intel (INTC) shares are down 9.4% after the company's bottom line in the second quarter missed Wall Street estimates. The firm's Q3 outlook also failed to impress investors. The company also announced a 15% workforce reduction.

data.title: Bayer's Elinzanetant review period gets extended by FDA
data.description:
Bayer (OTCPK:BAYZF) (OTCPK:BAYRY) (OTCPK:BAMXF) said it has received notification from the FDA regarding an extension of the review period for the marketing application of its experimental drug, Elinzanetant, to treat menopausal hot flash therapy.

The FDA has indicated that more time is necessary to thoroughly review the NDA submission, which includes additional information supporting the application.

The review period extension could last up to 90 days. The FDA has not expressed any concerns about the overall approval of Elinzanetant in its communications.

data.title: Henry Schein downgraded at Stifel on market share concerns
data.description:
Henry Schein (NASDAQ:HSIC) shares traded lower on Friday after Stifel downgraded the dental product distributor to Hold from Buy, citing potential market share losses for the company’s consumables business.

Analyst Jonathan Block cited an ongoing shift in the purchasing patterns for dental consumables. He pointed out that a Stifel-commissioned survey among general dentists indicated a maximum share of 31% for online vendors in the market for consumable distributor purchases, twice the current level.

In terms of market share impact, the analyst noted that Henry Schein (NASDAQ:HSIC) was the biggest loser in its survey, with most losses attributed to online channels. He projects HSIC’s current market share to drop by 130 bps to 35.2% over the next one to two years.

“Our checks suggest the online trend may continue for many more years, weighing on Henry Schein’s results,” the analyst wrote, trimming the stock’s target price to $75 from $80 per share.

As for valuation, Block argued that Henry Schein’s (NASDAQ:HSIC) premium in terms of NTM earnings per share is a “concern,” as the multiple stands above the stock’s three-year average and compared to its peers. “In light of our share diligence, the current premium is a concern,” he wrote.

data.title: First Western Financial outlines NIM expansion to mid-2.70s and targets continued operating leverage amid strong loan growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Western Financial, Inc. (MYFW) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Scott C. Wylie, Chairman and CEO, reported, "We executed well in the second quarter and saw positive trends in many areas, including loan and deposit growth, expansion in our net interest margin, well managed expenses and stable to improving asset quality." Wylie highlighted that loan production was well diversified and noted the impact of new banking team additions on productivity. He also emphasized the bank's ability to lower deposit costs and redeploy cash from OREO property sales into loans and securities, contributing to net interest margin expansion. The company used its strong capital position to repurchase shares during the quarter, resulting in an accretive effect on tangible book value per share.
  • Julie A. Courkamp, COO, stated, "Our loans held for investment increased $114 million from the end of the prior quarter." She noted new loan production of $167 million in Q2, with an average rate on new loans of 6.35% or 6.67% excluding certain trust-secured loans. Courkamp also discussed a $320 million increase in assets under management, citing favorable market performance.
  • David R. Weber, CFO, shared, "Our net interest income increased 2.3% from the prior quarter due to an expansion in our net interest margin. Our NIM increased 6 basis points from the prior quarter to 2.67%." He added that noninterest income decreased by approximately $1 million from the previous quarter due to onetime gains in Q1, partially offset by higher gain on sale of mortgage loans, and that noninterest expense decreased by approximately $300,000, primarily from lower salaries and benefits.

Outlook

  • Weber stated, "Based on recent deposit inflow trends over the past few weeks, we expect NIM to be relatively flat in the short term, but it should expand later in the year, which, along with our balance sheet growth, should result in strong NII growth in the third and fourth quarters."
  • Wylie noted, "Our loan and deposit pipelines remained strong and should continue to result in solid balance sheet growth for the second half of the year."
  • The company expects continued positive trends in net interest margin, net interest income, fee income, and more operating leverage resulting from disciplined expense control.

Financial Results

  • Wylie reported, "We generated net income of $2.5 million or $0.26 diluted share in the quarter. This was lower than the prior quarter due to a number of onetime gains that positively impacted our financial performance in the first quarter as well as the higher level of provision that we recorded due to the strong loan growth that we had late in the second quarter."
  • Weber specified the net interest margin increased to 2.67%, up from the prior quarter, and net interest income rose 2.3% sequentially. Total deposits were slightly up, with a decline in noninterest-bearing deposits offset by interest-bearing deposit growth. Noninterest expense fell by approximately $300,000. The company saw a $10 million decline in classified loans and reported one loan charge-off in the quarter.

Q&A

  • Matthew Timothy Clark, Piper Sandler: Asked about new borrowings and their rates. Weber responded, "Yes, they were overnight. And yes, the plan is to pay them off as our deposits come in, in the third quarter. It was in the mid-4s as far as the rate, but like I said, we do plan to pay those off."
  • Clark inquired about the spot rate on deposits and NIM relief expectations. Weber answered, "The spot rate at the end of June was 3.07%, and that's total deposits. And we do still have opportunity to continue to reprice down on the CD portfolio. As far as NIM expectations, we're thinking relatively flat in the third quarter...expecting NIM to expand in the fourth quarter back to really that exit NIM that we talked about last quarter kind of in the low to mid 2.70s."
  • Clark questioned expense guidance. Weber replied, "We're still thinking same range, $19.5 million to $20 million." Wylie added, "Our path to success is not in cost cutting, right? It's an operating leverage from growing revenues with our current expense base."
  • Wood Neblett Lay, KBW: Asked about NIM sensitivity to rate cuts. Weber responded, "Our guidance that we previously spoken to as far as how rate cuts impact NII in that $1 million range is still relatively fair. We took a little bit of sensitivity off the balance sheet in the second quarter. So maybe it's $100,000 or so below that."
  • Lay inquired about trust fees. Wylie explained the company has replaced most PTIM leadership and is launching a new B2B initiative, expecting future results.
  • William Joseph Dezellem, Tieton Capital Management: Asked about structural factors for achieving a 3%+ NIM. Wylie said, "We do think we're going to trend back there...I don't think we're going to get to 3.15% here in the next couple of quarters. I'm not sure whether it takes to the end of next year or beyond that, but we do think we're going to see continued progress there."
  • Dezellem asked about mortgage volume declines. Courkamp attributed this to market-wide uncertainty and interest rates, noting the business remains contribution positive.
  • Dezellem requested insight into client mindset. Wylie relayed that "the caution that we saw early in the year has shifted and people are coming back to doing business."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts probed for clarity on NIM expansion, expense control, and market outlook, with a slightly positive tone, reflecting cautious optimism and a desire for more specifics on sensitivity to rate changes and strategic priorities.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks and Q&A, consistently highlighting progress and future opportunities. Phrases such as "we do think we're going to trend back there" and "we expect to see results going forward" reinforced this outlook.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts appeared more optimistic, with increasing focus on growth and operating leverage.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 saw a notable increase in loan production and assets under management compared to Q1, which had flat loan balances and a decline in AUM. Net income was lower due to the absence of Q1's onetime gains and higher provision for loan growth.
  • The strategic focus remained on disciplined expense control and balance sheet growth, but Q2 emphasized new leadership in PTIM and a push toward trust fee growth, expanding beyond previous quarters' focus.
  • Management's tone was more upbeat about client activity and future growth, while analysts showed increased interest in NIM sensitivity and the impact of personnel changes.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited a highly competitive market for loans and deposits and noted continued pricing pressure.
  • Courkamp highlighted seasonal outflows in noninterest-bearing deposits related to tax payments but expected balances to rebuild.
  • Weber addressed the decrease in noninterest income and the downward trend in PTIM fees, with management prioritizing reversal.
  • Only one loan charge-off was reported, attributed to unique circumstances, not systemic asset quality issues.

Final Takeaway

First Western Financial, Inc. emphasized continued balance sheet growth, expanding net interest margin, and disciplined expense management as key drivers for future value creation. Leadership changes in trust and investment management and strategic initiatives in new business lines are expected to contribute to revenue diversification and operating leverage going forward. Management remains confident in the company's trajectory, supported by positive trends in loan production, deposit growth, and stable asset quality, setting the stage for further improvement in financial performance throughout the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Mid-caps are seeing a ‘golden cross’ that could mean +15% returns mid-to-long-term – Piper Sandler
data.description:
Mid-caps (SP400), (NYSEARCA:SPMD) are on the verge of a “golden cross” as its 50-day moving average has risen above its 200-day moving average, confirming an “underlying uptrend,” according to Craig W. Johnson, chief market technician at Piper Sandler.

He explained that investors have historically used the crossover between a 50-day and a 200-day moving average to identify trends. This “golden crossover” indicates a potential uptrend.

“Our research indicates that small gains followed this bullish signal in the short term,” Johnson said. “However, after four to eight weeks, returns incrementally improved by up to 15% over the intermediate-to-long term, with a high positivity rate.”

He added that, since 1991, there have been 22 times when this crossover occurred on mid-caps’ (SP400), (NYSEARCA:SPMD) investment cycle, with an average forward return of 10.3%, and a median return of 14.7%, resulting in a positivity rate of 73%.

 

 

Johnson also said his team is bullish on the equity markets due to a combination of “improving market breadth, an expansion in the number of industry groups making 26-week new highs, and many of the popular averages (SP500), (COMP:IND) reaching new highs.”

“This is particularly evident when looking at ‘SMID-cap’ stocks rather than the heavyweights in the S&P and Nasdaq indices,” he added. “We believe that this bull market still has legs to run into year-end.”

data.title: How Tractor Supply became the largest rural lifestyle retailer in the U.S.
data.description:
Tractor Supply Company (NASDAQ:TSCO) ranks in the top 20 of all retail chains in terms of store count as its growth story continues.

The company targets the rural lifestyle market, focusing primarily on recreational farmers, ranchers, horse owners, hobby and part-time farmers, suburban and rural homeowners, gardeners, pet enthusiasts, contractors, and tradesmen.

Tractor Supply (NASDAQ:TSCO) ended Q2 with 2,335 namesake stores open, compared to 2,254 at the end of the same quarter a year ago. The company also had 206 Petsense by Tractor Supply stores open, compared to 202 a year ago.

In the second quarter, the retailer opened 24 new Tractor Supply stores and 2 Petsense by Tractor Supply stores and closed 1 Petsense store. Tractor Supply (TSCO) management pointed to a robust pipeline of low-risk organic growth opportunities ahead. "Our recent acquisition of 18 Big Lots locations gives us great confidence to start 2026 with our new store pipeline in a strong position as we anticipate stepping up to 100 new stores next year. Over the past 2 years, we have significantly enhanced our real estate development," highlighted CEO Harry Lawton.

Tractor Supply Company (TSCO) began in 1938 as a mail-order business offering affordable tractor parts to farmers. The first retail store opened in Minot, North Dakota, in 1939. Over the following decades, the company steadily expanded, opening stores throughout the Midwest and eventually nationwide. 

By 2011, Tractor Supply had grown to 1,000 stores. Rapid expansion continued, and the 2,000th store opened just ten years later, in 2021. Today, Tractor Supply operates stores across 49 states, making it the largest rural lifestyle retail chain in the United States. The company has set a target for 3,200 total stores in the coming years.

Analysts have pointed to the company's strong track record of picking out locations for new stores in a steady and strategic fashion, which has led to consistent earnings growth.

Shares of Tractor Supply (TSCO) are up 11.8% on a year-to-date basis amid some concerns about valuation.

data.title: Sony makes a $460M investment in Bandai Namco to boost anime and manga content
data.description:
• Sony Group Corporation (NYSE:SONY) is taking a 2.5% stake in Bandai Namco (OTCPK:NCBDF) for over $460M (68B yen).
  • Sony is buying the equity stake by purchasing 16M shares of the game publisher, which owns popular titles like Pac-Man and Tekken.
  • The companies said they have agreed to focus on expanding the fan community for IP such as anime and manga around the world.
  • They also plan to engage in detailed discussions regarding the joint development and promotion of IP, in addition to planning and technical collaboration on experiential entertainment. 
  • Sony and Bandai Namco said they plan to explore opportunities to support creators by jointly developing and operating entertainment-related technologies and services, and to engage in joint investments and business alliances in fan engagement areas.
  • Press Release

data.title: Gentex outlines $2.44B-$2.61B 2025 revenue target as VOXX acquisition drives product, margin initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Gentex Corporation (GNTX) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Steven R. Downing, President and CEO, highlighted the completion of the VOXX acquisition on April 1 and differentiated core Gentex from consolidated Gentex, noting "consolidated net sales were $657.9 million, which represents a 15% increase over the second quarter of last year." He emphasized the impact of tariffs on China sales, stating sales into China were approximately $33 million for the quarter versus a forecast of $50 million to $60 million, but pointed to strong performance in Full Display Mirror and advanced features, along with VOXX's contribution.
  • Downing reported, "Our consolidated gross margin for the quarter was 34.2%, up from 32.9% in the second quarter of last year," with core Gentex gross margin at 35.3%. He credited "purchasing cost reductions, favorable product mix and operational efficiencies" for the margin improvement.
  • Downing added, "Consolidated income from operations was $118.5 million compared to $114.9 million last year. However, core Gentex operating income was $123.8 million, up 8% year-over-year."
  • Kevin C. Nash, CFO, said, "Gentex automotive net sales were $566.5 million in the second quarter of '25, which were negatively affected by the company's lower-than-expected sales into the China market due to the impact of counter tariffs, but were more than offset by increased advanced feature mirror sales."
  • Nash highlighted the company's capital allocation, stating, "the company repurchased 5.7 million shares of its common stock at an average price of $22.13 per share for a total of $126.2 million."
  • Neil Boehm, COO & CTO, reported, "In the second quarter of 2025, we had 18 net new nameplate launches... Over half of these launches in the quarter included advanced feature content with Full Display Mirror and HomeLink being the primary technologies introduced." Boehm projected Full Display Mirror unit shipments to increase by 150,000 to 300,000 units in 2025 versus 2024 and announced the launch of the PLACE product line through a major big box retailer.

Outlook

  • Downing revised full-year 2025 consolidated revenue guidance, stating, "We now expect consolidated revenue, including VOXX to be in the range of $2.44 billion and $2.61 billion, which is higher than our previous estimate of $2.15 billion and $2.32 billion without VOXX."
  • Revenue from Gentex's primary markets is projected at $2.1 billion to $2.2 billion, with China at $100 million to $125 million, and VOXX contributing $240 million to $280 million.
  • Consolidated gross margin is expected to be between 33% and 34%, with core Gentex at 34% to 34.5%. VOXX gross margin is anticipated at 27% to 29%.
  • Operating expenses, excluding severance, are forecasted at $370 million to $390 million, with core Gentex expenses unchanged at $300 million to $310 million.
  • Downing stated, "Our effective tax rate is now expected to be in the range of 16% to 17%."

Financial Results

  • Consolidated net sales were $657.9 million, with core Gentex revenue at $579 million. VOXX contributed $78.8 million in sales.
  • Net income for the quarter was $96 million, up from $86 million last year. Adjusted net income was $105.8 million.
  • Consolidated earnings per share were $0.43, and adjusted EPS was $0.47 after one-time expenses.
  • Operating expenses rose to $106.8 million, with $23.9 million attributed to VOXX, plus acquisition and severance costs. Adjusted core Gentex operating expenses were down slightly versus last year.
  • Cash and cash equivalents were $119.8 million, with total investments at $290.1 million.
  • Capital expenditures were $31.1 million for the quarter, and depreciation and amortization totaled $27.4 million.

Q&A

  • Luke L. Junk, Baird: Asked about gross margin drivers and sustainability; Downing explained "there was a negative in the quarter between pricing and tariffs of 50 to 100 basis points... the biggest ones were PPV... 100 to 150 basis points. And then labor and overhead savings were 150 to 200 basis points positive."
  • Junk: Asked about China strategy; Downing said the "biggest challenge in the China market is... the threat of those counter tariffs when they got over 100%... most of it got resourced to local suppliers, but the vast majority... just removed it from the vehicle."
  • Joseph Robert Spak, UBS: Questioned VOXX cost synergies; Downing replied, "We know there's some synergies in combining the 2 organizations... This is a 12- to 18-month process."
  • Spak: Asked about VOXX audio supply chain; Downing said, "the Klipsch and Onkyo teams have done a phenomenal job of proactively getting sourcing decisions made to reduce that risk."
  • Spak: Inquired about production cadence; Downing stated, "Q3 is going to be very similar to Q2... softening is really going to happen in Q4."
  • Michael Joshua Nichols, B. Riley: Asked about mix and advanced features; Downing noted "return to where we were a couple of years ago, where OEMs were focused on profitability... that does tend to shift part of the upper half of the production volume into higher mix for us."
  • Nichols: Asked about VOXX margin improvement; Downing said, "if you're looking out 2 years or so, I think 200 to 300 basis points of improvement in the gross margin is absolutely achievable."
  • Nichols: Asked about driver monitoring revenue ramp; Boehm confirmed "volumes will be ramping up over the next couple of years, really '27 into '28."
  • Ronald John Jewsikow, Guggenheim: Sought color on FDM growth; Downing described "combination of... launch cadence and both take rates." Jewsikow also pressed on China; Downing attributed most volume loss to "decontenting... driven by the profitability of the OEMs in that market."
  • Jewsikow: Questioned net tariff costs; Downing clarified, "It was about $2.7 million."
  • David Whiston, Morningstar: Asked about margin targets; Downing stated, "if mix stayed the same, I would tell you we're there. But we have additional things that we had kicked off that we're still working on."
  • Whiston: Inquired about rare earths exposure; Downing acknowledged "quite a bit of exposure on the coating side for core Gentex... Klipsch acquisition, magnets became a much bigger piece."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on gross margin sustainability, China market strategy, VOXX integration, product mix, and future growth drivers. The tone was largely neutral, with some slightly negative undertones regarding China and tariffs, as analysts sought clarity on recurring risks and margin headwinds.
  • Management conveyed confidence in cost control and margin improvement, using phrases such as "I think is a really positive sign" and "team is doing a phenomenal job," but also acknowledged ongoing risks and uncertainty, especially in China and with tariffs. During Q&A, management responses remained detailed, with Downing frequently providing explanations and breakdowns.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment shifted from concern over tariff impacts and revenue headwinds to a more nuanced focus on margin resilience and operational execution. Management’s tone was more upbeat regarding margin progress and VOXX integration, yet still cautious about macroeconomic and China-related risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from a narrower focus on core Gentex and tariff uncertainty in Q1 to broader, consolidated figures with VOXX included in Q2. Revenue guidance was increased with the addition of VOXX, while gross margin targets for core Gentex were raised from 33%-34% to 34%-34.5%.
  • Strategic focus expanded to include VOXX product integration, operational synergies, and new product launches like PLACE for the smart home market.
  • Analysts in Q2 homed in on margin drivers and integration synergies, compared to more acute tariff and China concerns in Q1.
  • Management’s confidence increased on margin recovery and integration progress, while maintaining caution on China and tariffs.
  • Product pipeline updates grew more detailed, with greater emphasis on Full Display Mirror, large area devices, and consumer-facing lines.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing challenges from tariffs and reduced China demand, with Downing stating "sales into China... were approximately $33 million compared to our beginning of year forecast of $50 million to $60 million."
  • The company is working on mitigating tariff exposure, seeking reimbursement for unreimbursed costs, and diversifying supply chains, especially in the VOXX audio segment.
  • Analysts repeatedly raised questions about the sustainability of margin gains, exposure to China, and integration of VOXX, with management outlining ongoing cost control and operational initiatives.

Final Takeaway

Gentex closed the quarter with a solid performance in revenue and margins, supported by the VOXX acquisition and disciplined cost controls. The company raised its full-year guidance, signaling confidence in margin recovery and operational synergies while acknowledging persistent risks from tariffs and China market weakness. Investments in new product launches and technology platforms, such as Full Display Mirror and smart home solutions, remain strategic priorities as Gentex advances its growth and profitability targets for 2025 and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Dow Inc. adds to losses near 52-week low as Evercore cuts after weaker than expected Q2
data.description:
Dow Inc. (NYSE:DOW) -1% in Friday's trading, extending the stock's two-day decline to nearly 20% and within $0.05/share of its 52-week low, after reporting a sharp Q2 loss, deteriorating margins and a surprise dividend cut.

Evercore ISI analysts led by Eric Boyes downgraded Dow (NYSE:DOW) to In-Line from Outperform with a $32 price target, slashed from $56, saying the company's dividend cut "adds flexibility, but shifts focus to sustainability of the lackluster outlook and lack of visibility on recovery."

The dividend action was "necessary, but a 50% reduction leaves us wondering if this was sufficient in light of a lower-for-longer setup for polyolefins" in 2026 and beyond, and "organically covering now $1Bn dividends plus $2B-$2.5B in capex assuming ~70% cash conversion requires investors to underwrite $4.3B-$5Bn in EBITDA in 2026 - "plausible, but... far from assured and certain to be debated," according to Boyes.

"There is a lower-for-longer backdrop with little over the horizon to suggest improvement, and a tepid [dividend] cut level plus 2H outlook that is unlikely to restore confidence in the immediate term" has led to the downgrade, the analyst wrote.

data.title: Buenaventura outlines San Gabriel project 88% complete, targets first gold bar by Q4 2025 while accelerating copper sales
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Compañía de Minas Buenaventura S.A.A. (BVN) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Leandro Luis Martin Garcia Raggio, CEO, opened by noting, "Our EBITDA in the second quarter 2025 from direct operations was $130 million compared to $107 million reported in the second quarter of 2024. Our net income in the second quarter of 2025 was $91 million compared to the $71 million in the net income -- in the second quarter of 2024."
  • The CEO highlighted that silver production reached 3.6 million ounces, "11% lower compared to the 4 million ounces produced during the same period in last year," while copper production increased 28% year-over-year, primarily due to resumed operations at El Brocal.
  • Gold production for the period was 27,345 ounces. The CEO stated Buenaventura had initiated the sale of part of Cerro Verde's copper concentrate, selling approximately 20,000 metric tonnes by quarter's end with another 20,000 tonnes expected for the full year.
  • The CEO reported, "Cerro Verde announced a new dividend distribution of $59 million on July 24, corresponding to Buenaventura's equity share. These dividends will be distributed in August, resulting in a total of $108 million."
  • The total CapEx for the quarter was $107 million, with $82 million allocated to the San Gabriel project. Cash at quarter end was $589 million and total debt $860 million, resulting in a leverage ratio of 0.56x. "On July 23, Buenaventura redeemed the remaining $149 million of its 2026 bond."
  • The CEO also indicated, "San Gabriel cumulative progress reaching 88% overall completion by the second quarter of 2025, primarily driven by finishing the engineering and procurement as well as the construction -- is at 86% of advance."
  • Daniel Dominguez Vera, CFO, added, "The total amount of the CapEx for San Gabriel will be in the order of $720 million to $750 million. And we have already disbursed close to $600 million as of June 2025."

Outlook

  • The CEO reaffirmed, "We anticipate commencing the ramp-up pace in the third quarter of 2025, followed by the production of the first gold bar in the fourth quarter of 2025. However, these milestones remain subject to the timely approval to the necessary permits."
  • Management expects to disburse between $130 million and $160 million for San Gabriel construction by year-end.
  • The company targets stabilizing San Gabriel production after ramp-up, aiming for "between 100,000 ounces and 120,000 ounces" of annual gold output.
  • No explicit changes to guidance compared to the prior quarter were announced.

Financial Results

  • The quarter saw EBITDA from direct operations of $130 million and net income of $91 million.
  • Silver production fell to 3.6 million ounces, while copper production increased and gold output was 27,345 ounces.
  • Buenaventura initiated sales of Cerro Verde copper concentrate, contributing additional revenue streams.
  • CapEx totaled $107 million, with significant allocation to San Gabriel.
  • The company ended the quarter with $589 million in cash and a leverage ratio of 0.56x.
  • All-in sustaining cost for copper increased by 63% year-over-year, primarily due to lower by-product credits.

Q&A

  • Carlos De Alba, Morgan Stanley: Asked for more color on San Gabriel's pending permits, CapEx for the remainder of 2025, and expected ramp-up timeline. CEO Garcia explained that the permit is a "very simple permit that is the actual permit to begin the throughput," with no major risk anticipated. Dominguez, CFO, clarified expected remaining CapEx. Ortiz Zevallos, VP of Operations, gave a detailed account of ramp-up plans, noting, "The processing plant is progressing... in October, we will start the wet commissioning with slurry."
  • Cesar Perez-Novoa, BTG Pactual: Queried on the stabilization of silver grades at Uchucchacua and strategy for higher ore mining at El Brocal. Ortiz Zevallos explained that seismic activity required backfilling and that ramp-up to 2,000 tonnes per day is expected by year-end, with cost reductions anticipated.
  • Tanya M. Jakusconek, Scotiabank: Inquired about modeling copper concentrate sales, continuation of the process, and timing on Trapiche environmental permits and feasibility study. Massa, VP of Business Development, explained, "We already have sold 20,000 tonnes... and our idea is to sell 20,000 tonnes more in the second half." Hermoza and Macher detailed that Trapiche's environmental impact study is expected to conclude by year-end and feasibility study mid-2026.
  • Jakusconek also sought clarification on San Gabriel's stockpile and ramp-up to commercial production. Ortiz Zevallos noted stockpile targets are being revised but expect "maybe 200,000" tonnes before commissioning, with steady-state production targeted for the second half of 2026.
  • A webcast question from Omar asked about road blockades and use of Cerro Verde dividend. CEO Garcia said operations were unaffected and funds would be used for CapEx and possibly dividends.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was largely neutral to slightly positive, focused on operational updates and guidance for San Gabriel, with pressing questions on project milestones and cost trends.
  • Management maintained a confident and detailed tone during prepared remarks and Q&A, often addressing potential risks directly and providing operational specifics. The CEO stated, "We don't foresee a greater risk in that permit."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained steady, with analysts showing continued interest in San Gabriel's execution and cost structure.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for San Gabriel production, project CapEx, and timeline remains largely unchanged from Q1, though progress has advanced from 79% to 88% completion.
  • CapEx spending increased significantly from $36 million in Q1 to $107 million in Q2, mainly for San Gabriel.
  • The leverage ratio increased from 0.46x to 0.56x as cash position declined with increased CapEx outflows.
  • All-in sustaining cost for copper reversed from an 83% decrease in Q1 to a 63% increase in Q2, attributed to lower by-product credits.
  • Analyst focus in both quarters was centered on San Gabriel, cost management, and operational stability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Pending permit approval for San Gabriel's commissioning remains a gating factor for ramp-up and gold production in Q4 2025.
  • Seismic activity at Uchucchacua required halting work in certain areas and implementing additional backfilling, with operations expected to normalize by Q4.
  • The narrow valley for tailings at San Gabriel presents a constraint for initial ramp-up, with management planning a cautious approach.
  • All-in sustaining cost increases and lower by-product credits could impact profitability if trends persist.

Final Takeaway

Management reiterates its commitment to timely San Gabriel delivery, maintaining financial stability, and leveraging new copper concentrate sales for incremental revenue. The company expects to initiate San Gabriel's ramp-up in Q3 2025, targeting first gold bar production by Q4, while closely managing operational challenges and sustaining exploration and CapEx investments to support future growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
